principles.directive: Use reduced complexity as the primary success metric and optimize for local understandability rather than size, line count, or cleverness.
principles.source: `Purpose` (3-19), `Primary Directive` (21-34), `Core Complexity Rules` (36-54), `Review Checklist` (345-357), `Final Instruction` (361-370).

principles.directive: Treat design as continuous work; do not accept a working patch that worsens future changeability, and compare plausible alternatives for non-trivial design choices.
principles.source: `Strategic Programming over Tactical Programming` (118-131), `Design Alternatives and Comments-First Design` (246-257), `Review Checklist` (345-357).

principles.directive: Prefer deep modules and reject shallow wrappers, pass-through services, thin library wrappers, helper modules, and tiny split-outs that add names without reducing reader burden.
principles.source: `Module Depth Rules` (57-77), `Combine or Separate Code` (231-242), `Forbidden Patterns / Shallow Decomposition` (297-302), `Code Generation Rules` (317-332).

principles.directive: Design interfaces around caller knowledge rather than implementation mechanics; avoid fragile staging, setup sequences, flags, and arguments that expose internal choices.
principles.source: `Interface Design Rules` (96-114), `Code Generation Rules` (317-332), `Review Checklist` (345-357).

principles.directive: Hide volatile decisions, representations, storage shape, protocols, file formats, performance hacks, bookkeeping, normalization, and messy edge handling inside the owning module.
principles.source: `Information Hiding Rules` (81-92), `Forbidden Patterns / Interface Leakage` (303-306), `Code Generation Rules` (317-332).

principles.directive: Pull complexity downward when the lower module owns the detail, even if implementation grows slightly, so callers get a simpler public contract and less repeated reasoning.
principles.source: `Pull Complexity Downward` (159-166), `Forbidden Patterns / Complexity Spread` (311-314), `Code Generation Rules` (317-332).

principles.directive: Choose generality at the right level and isolate special behavior instead of overfitting, speculating, or polluting the core path with edge cases.
principles.source: `General-Purpose vs Special-Purpose Modules` (135-140), `Special-General Decomposition` (218-227), `Error Handling and Exception Elimination` (144-155).

principles.directive: Combine or split by total complexity, not size, runtime order, habit, or aesthetics; keep related state, behavior, invariants, and design decisions together unless the new boundary is deeper.
principles.source: `Function and Variable Rules` (189-200), `Temporal Decomposition Rules` (204-214), `Combine or Separate Code` (231-242).

principles.directive: Reduce exception surface by changing interfaces or invariants where possible instead of making every caller repeat defensive ceremony.
principles.source: `Error Handling and Exception Elimination` (144-155), `Special-General Decomposition` (218-227), `Forbidden Patterns / Tactical Complexity Debt` (307-310).

principles.directive: Use comments to reduce complexity through contracts, invariants, hidden design decisions, rationale, and tricky facts; do not narrate code or compensate for bad names, poor decomposition, or confusing flow.
principles.source: `Comment Rules` (170-185), `Design Alternatives and Comments-First Design` (250-257), `Review Rules` (281-294).

principles.directive: Treat names, consistency, and obviousness as design information; reveal abstractions rather than mechanisms and treat surprising code as complexity.
principles.source: `Interface Design Rules` (98-102), `Naming, Consistency, and Obviousness` (261-267), `Function and Variable Rules` (191-200).

principles.directive: Use tests to protect public behavior, interface contracts, hidden complexity through stable APIs, and isolated special cases; do not let test convenience force shallow or leaky interfaces.
principles.source: `Performance, Trends, and Tests` (277-278), `Testing Rules` (336-342).

principles.directive: Add performance optimizations, trends, paradigms, patterns, or frameworks only when they reduce complexity in this codebase or evidence shows the tradeoff matters; hide optimization details behind stable interfaces.
principles.source: `Information Hiding Rules` (86), `Performance, Trends, and Tests` (271-278).

principles.directive: When a feature feels awkward, one change spreads across files, or reviewers must reconstruct hidden dependencies, look for missing information hiding, shallow modules, temporal coupling, or complexity pushed to callers.
principles.source: `Core Complexity Rules` (36-54), `Information Hiding Rules` (81-92), `Review Rules` (281-294), `Forbidden Patterns / Complexity Spread` (311-314).

principles.directive: When adding a module, layer, service, helper, wrapper, facade, pattern, option, callback, or argument, prove that it hides more complexity than it adds.
principles.source: `Module Depth Rules` (57-77), `Interface Design Rules` (100-114), `Forbidden Patterns` (297-314), `Code Generation Rules` (317-332).

principles.directive: When touching an API, check whether ordinary callers must know sequencing, representation, storage, transport, caching, protocol, file format, internal workflow, or too many setup steps.
principles.source: `Information Hiding Rules` (83-92), `Interface Design Rules` (98-114), `Forbidden Patterns / Interface Leakage` (303-306).

principles.directive: When adding a special case, flag, exception path, conditional, or exposed container, first ask whether the owning module can eliminate the invalid state, isolate unusual behavior, or provide a stronger operation.
principles.source: `Interface Design Rules` (110-114), `Error Handling and Exception Elimination` (144-155), `Special-General Decomposition` (218-227), `Forbidden Patterns` (307-314).

principles.directive: When splitting, extracting, or introducing variables, check whether the new boundary or name captures meaning or only adds jumps, pass-through state, and visible intermediate steps.
principles.source: `Function and Variable Rules` (189-200), `Combine or Separate Code` (231-242).

principles.directive: When code is organized as `prepare/process/finalize`, staged objects, or execution-order phases, verify that temporal structure is the real concept; otherwise reorganize around stable responsibilities.
principles.source: `Temporal Decomposition Rules` (204-214), `Combine or Separate Code` (239-242).

principles.directive: When naming is vague, mechanism-focused, inconsistent, or surprising, reconsider the abstraction boundary instead of accepting a near miss.
principles.source: `Interface Design Rules` (102), `Naming, Consistency, and Obviousness` (261-267).

principles.directive: When comments get long, duplicate code, justify a confusing interface, or explain usage by exposing internals, redesign the abstraction or move the missing contract to the interface.
principles.source: `Comment Rules` (170-185), `Design Alternatives and Comments-First Design` (250-257), `Review Rules` (292).

principles.directive: When optimizing performance, measure first and hide the optimization; do not sacrifice module depth or information hiding without evidence that the tradeoff matters.
principles.source: `Information Hiding Rules` (86), `Performance, Trends, and Tests` (271-275).

principles.directive: When testing or reviewing, focus on public behavior, interface contracts, hidden complexity through stable APIs, and special cases isolated behind the abstraction.
principles.source: `Review Rules` (281-294), `Testing Rules` (336-342).

principles.directive: Preserve independent business rules, inward dependencies, testability, and replaceable details even when the immediate feature would be shorter without them.
principles.source: `Purpose` (3-17), `Architecture Economics and Priority` (257-264), `Final Instruction` (506-515).

principles.directive: Source dependencies must point inward toward higher-level policy; core policy must not import volatile details.
principles.source: `Non-Negotiable Rules` (21-24, 66-70), `Required Layer Responsibilities` (85-96, 106-115, 125-152), `Architecture Heuristics` (214-221), `Paradigm and Component Rules` (270-280), `Forbidden Patterns` (377-380), `Preferred Default Shapes` (487-490), `Final Instruction` (506-515).

principles.directive: Put enterprise rules and invariants in entities or equivalent domain objects; put application-specific orchestration in focused use cases.
principles.source: `Non-Negotiable Rules` (26-29, 56-64), `Required Layer Responsibilities` (76-115), `Architecture Heuristics` (223-230), `Output Expectations` (421-427), `Review Checklist` (448-453).

principles.directive: Pass plain request and response models across use-case boundaries; do not pass framework, web, ORM, database, or response objects into or out of core policy.
principles.source: `Non-Negotiable Rules` (26-44), `Application Layer` (98-115), `Code Generation Rules` (160-180), `Forbidden Patterns` (352-360), `Preferred Default Shapes` (480-485).

principles.directive: Treat frameworks, databases, web delivery, messaging, filesystems, clocks, service clients, networks, devices, and vendors as outer-layer details behind ports, gateways, presenters, mappers, or adapters.
principles.source: `Non-Negotiable Rules` (31-49), `Required Layer Responsibilities` (117-152), `Code Generation Rules` (182-208), `Architecture Heuristics` (232-241), `Services, Distribution, and Embedded Boundaries` (296-302), `Refactoring Rules` (396-405), `Output Expectations` (421-427).

principles.directive: Inner layers own the interfaces they need; outer layers implement them; concrete wiring belongs in the composition root or outer-layer main component.
principles.source: `Non-Negotiable Rules` (46-70), `Infrastructure Layer` (134-152), `Code Generation Rules` (182-203), `Preferred Default Shapes` (487-490).

principles.directive: Keep adapters humble: translate external formats to use-case calls and back instead of owning business decisions.
principles.source: `Non-Negotiable Rules` (41-44), `Interface Adapters Layer` (117-132), `Infrastructure Layer` (134-152), `Services, Distribution, and Embedded Boundaries` (296-302), `Forbidden Patterns` (362-375), `Output Expectations` (434-440).

principles.directive: Organize by use case, feature, or business capability before generic technical buckets.
principles.source: `Non-Negotiable Rules` (51-59), `Architecture Heuristics` (243-253), `Naming Rules` (306-313), `Output Expectations` (421-427), `Review Checklist` (457-459), `Preferred Default Shapes` (468-478).

principles.directive: Choose boundaries by volatility, policy importance, substitution value, testability, and cost; use the lightest enforceable boundary, including partial boundaries, when full separation is too expensive.
principles.source: `Code Generation Rules` (205-208), `Architecture Economics and Priority` (257-264), `Boundary Cost, Deployment, and Operations` (284-292), `When Tradeoffs Are Necessary` (494-502).

principles.directive: Do not merge unrelated use cases or eliminate duplication when sharing would couple actors, change reasons, team ownership, deployment needs, or release pressure.
principles.source: `Architecture Economics and Priority` (264), `Paradigm and Component Rules` (273-280), `Boundary Cost, Deployment, and Operations` (289-291), `Naming Rules` (306-313), `Forbidden Patterns` (367-385).

principles.directive: Use structured code, dependency inversion, role-sized interfaces, substitutable implementations, controlled mutation, acyclic components, and stability-directed dependencies to protect policy from volatile details.
principles.source: `Paradigm and Component Rules` (268-280), `Code Generation Rules` (200-203), `Forbidden Patterns` (377-385).

principles.directive: Enforce boundaries with package structure, dependency rules, build constraints, tests, visibility, or narrow APIs.
principles.source: `Code Generation Rules` (200-208), `Boundary Cost, Deployment, and Operations` (286-292), `Services, Distribution, and Embedded Boundaries` (298-302), `Forbidden Patterns` (372-385).

principles.directive: Test entities, use cases, and boundary contracts first, without real volatile details; test adapters separately at the seams.
principles.source: `Non-Negotiable Rules` (36-39), `Testing Rules` (317-344), `Services, Distribution, and Embedded Boundaries` (301-302), `Refactoring Rules` (410-411), `Review Checklist` (456).

principles.directive: Preserve behavior while improving dependency direction; prefer incremental boundary extraction over rewrites and call out architectural debt when it cannot be fixed safely now.
principles.source: `Refactoring Rules` (389-415), `Output Expectations` (429-440), `When Tradeoffs Are Necessary` (494-502).

principles.directive: When urgent delivery would skip architecture, state the future change, test, replacement, or operational cost before accepting the shortcut.
principles.source: `Architecture Economics and Priority` (257-264), `When Tradeoffs Are Necessary` (494-502), `Final Instruction` (506-515).

principles.directive: When framework annotations, request/response objects, serializers, ORM rows, schemas, vendor SDKs, config, environment reads, device registers, or transport formats enter core policy, move translation outward.
principles.source: `Non-Negotiable Rules` (26-49), `Required Layer Responsibilities` (85-152), `Code Generation Rules` (177-180), `Forbidden Patterns` (352-360), `Services, Distribution, and Embedded Boundaries` (301-302).

principles.directive: When controllers, jobs, handlers, views, presenters, gateways, repositories, SQL, service listeners, scripts, or hardware adapters contain business branching or validation, move the rule inward.
principles.source: `Non-Negotiable Rules` (56-64), `Interface Adapters Layer` (117-132), `Infrastructure Layer` (149-152), `Services, Distribution, and Embedded Boundaries` (298-302), `Forbidden Patterns` (357-370), `Refactoring Rules` (393-405).

principles.directive: When a use case instantiates infrastructure, calls a volatile dependency directly, or depends on a concrete implementation, introduce a policy-owned port and wire the concrete detail at the edge.
principles.source: `Code Generation Rules` (182-203), `Forbidden Patterns` (377-380), `Preferred Default Shapes` (487-490).

principles.directive: When a `*Service`, utility folder, shared module, base package, or generic `core` package becomes an escape hatch, split by use case, role, or ownership and restore dependency direction.
principles.source: `Non-Negotiable Rules` (51-59), `Code Generation Rules` (200-203), `Naming Rules` (306-313), `Forbidden Patterns` (367-385), `Refactoring Rules` (406-408).

principles.directive: When an adapter bypasses a use case, a presenter reads persistence directly, or infrastructure is both imported by and importing inward code, restore the intended boundary.
principles.source: `Interface Adapters Layer` (117-132), `Architecture Heuristics` (214-221), `Forbidden Patterns` (372-380), `Review Checklist` (448-460).

principles.directive: When service boundaries, process boundaries, remote calls, deployment boundaries, or embedded hardware appear, still verify source dependencies, data ownership, I/O cost, and policy independence.
principles.source: `Boundary Cost, Deployment, and Operations` (284-292), `Services, Distribution, and Embedded Boundaries` (296-302), `Final Instruction` (508-513).

principles.directive: When tests need volatile details to verify business rules, move tests to use cases/entities with fakes or add a stable boundary contract.
principles.source: `Testing Rules` (317-344), `Services, Distribution, and Embedded Boundaries` (301-302), `Refactoring Rules` (410-411), `Review Checklist` (456).

principles.directive: When a compromise is unavoidable, keep it at the outermost layer possible, document the violation, avoid normalizing it, and preserve a path to separation.
principles.source: `When Tradeoffs Are Necessary` (494-502), `Output Expectations` (429-432), `Final Instruction` (506-515).

principles.directive: Treat cleanliness as part of delivery; preserve behavior, leave touched code cleaner within scope, and reject schedule or rewrite excuses for new mess.
principles.source: `Priority and behavior` (5-12), `Core clean code principles` (14-22), `Refactoring rules` (188-197), `Emergent design and successive refinement` (199-205), `Hard rules` (290-297).

principles.directive: Write for local reasoning so readers do not reconstruct hidden state, wide jumps, or naming trivia.
principles.source: `Core clean code principles` (14-22), `Formatting and structure` (81-91), `Class and module design` (104-113), `Implementation preferences` (255-264), `Review checklist` (266-279).

principles.directive: Use precise names and one term per concept; rename when vocabulary hides intent, overloads meaning, or forces comments to compensate.
principles.source: `Naming rules` (24-42), `Function rules` (44-62), `Comment rules` (64-79), `Refactoring rules` (188-197), `Smells to detect and eliminate` (207-240), `Change Process` (242-253), `Hard rules` (290-297).

principles.directive: Keep functions small, focused, top-down, and at one abstraction level.
principles.source: `Function rules` (44-62), `Formatting and structure` (81-91), `Smells to detect and eliminate` (207-240), `Review checklist` (266-279).

principles.directive: Keep parameters few and meaningful; avoid boolean flags, output parameters, and grab-bag argument lists by modeling the concept.
principles.source: `Function rules` (44-62), `Smells to detect and eliminate` (207-240).

principles.directive: Separate commands from queries and eliminate hidden side effects.
principles.source: `Function rules` (44-62), `Smells to detect and eliminate` (207-240), `Hard rules` (290-297).

principles.directive: Keep the happy path readable; isolate error handling, invalid-state handling, and cleanup; prefer explicit optionality or typed results over null-like sentinel flow when supported.
principles.source: `Function rules` (44-62), `Error handling` (115-124), `Concurrency and async work` (172-186), `Review checklist` (266-279).

principles.directive: Expose behavior rather than raw representation and avoid train-wreck access, utility dumping grounds, and mixed responsibilities.
principles.source: `Objects, modules, and data structures` (93-102), `Class and module design` (104-113), `Smells to detect and eliminate` (207-240).

principles.directive: Keep construction, framework, persistence, transaction, security, and vendor details outside business behavior.
principles.source: `Objects, modules, and data structures` (93-102), `Boundaries and external dependencies` (126-132), `System construction rules` (134-143), `Implementation preferences` (255-264).

principles.directive: Make public APIs small, explicit, and hard to misuse; encode boundary logic, required order, and likely changes where readers can see them.
principles.source: `Objects, modules, and data structures` (93-102), `Class and module design` (104-113), `Error handling` (115-124), `Boundaries and external dependencies` (126-132), `Implementation preferences` (255-264).

principles.directive: Use comments only for rationale, constraints, warnings, or external contracts; never as a bandage for bad names or structure.
principles.source: `Naming rules` (24-42), `Comment rules` (64-79), `Change Process` (242-253), `Review checklist` (266-279), `Hard rules` (290-297).

principles.directive: Treat tests as production code and back changes with proportionate validation before calling them done.
principles.source: `Tests` (145-157), `TDD and clean test rules` (159-170), `Change Process` (242-253), `Review checklist` (266-279), `Output Expectations` (281-288).

principles.directive: Let design emerge through tests, duplication removal, expressiveness, and minimal structure; avoid needless abstractions and infrastructure.
principles.source: `Priority and behavior` (5-12), `System construction rules` (134-143), `Refactoring rules` (188-197), `Emergent design and successive refinement` (199-205), `Implementation preferences` (255-264).

principles.directive: When touching code, remove the smell that most increases change cost without silently broadening the task beyond the smallest cleanup that keeps the change safe.
principles.source: `Priority and behavior` (5-12), `Refactoring rules` (188-197), `Emergent design and successive refinement` (199-205), `Smells to detect and eliminate` (207-240), `Change Process` (242-253), `Hard rules` (290-297).

principles.directive: When a function mixes setup, validation, computation, and side effects, split the phases.
principles.source: `Function rules` (44-62), `Smells to detect and eliminate` (207-240).

principles.directive: When a comment explains control flow, simplify names or structure before keeping the comment.
principles.source: `Comment rules` (64-79), `Change Process` (242-253), `Hard rules` (290-297).

principles.directive: When a function both mutates and answers, or hides a mode switch behind a flag, separate the responsibilities.
principles.source: `Function rules` (44-62), `Smells to detect and eliminate` (207-240), `Hard rules` (290-297).

principles.directive: When duplication, repeated switches, or primitive clusters appear, name the concept with an argument object, polymorphism, special case, or another small abstraction.
principles.source: `Function rules` (44-62), `Refactoring rules` (188-197), `Emergent design and successive refinement` (199-205), `Smells to detect and eliminate` (207-240).

principles.directive: When a boundary leaks framework, vendor, or persistence quirks inward, add or strengthen a local adapter.
principles.source: `Objects, modules, and data structures` (93-102), `Boundaries and external dependencies` (126-132), `System construction rules` (134-143), `Implementation preferences` (255-264).

principles.directive: When async or concurrency enters, isolate threading policy, minimize shared mutable state, define shutdown, and test timing-sensitive behavior.
principles.source: `Error handling` (115-124), `Concurrency and async work` (172-186), `Smells to detect and eliminate` (207-240).

principles.directive: When fixing a bug or changing behavior, add or update the test that protects the intended contract.
principles.source: `Tests` (145-157), `TDD and clean test rules` (159-170), `Change Process` (242-253), `Review checklist` (266-279).

principles.directive: When cleanup starts spreading into unrelated areas, cut back to the smallest refactor that keeps the requested change safe and readable.
principles.source: `Priority and behavior` (5-12), `Refactoring rules` (188-197), `Emergent design and successive refinement` (199-205), `Change Process` (242-253), `Hard rules` (290-297).

principles.directive: Before large construction work, verify requirements, architecture, major risks, coding conventions, language constraints, error policy, data representation, reuse, integration, and testing approach.
principles.source: `Construction Prerequisites and Decisions` (45-52), `Purpose` (3-16), `Review Checklist` (327-341).

principles.directive: When upstream uncertainty remains, build a small validated slice instead of speculative code, and make expensive-to-reverse decisions deliberately.
principles.source: `Construction Prerequisites and Decisions` (45-52), `Primary Directive` (20-31).

principles.directive: Optimize for human readers: clarity, locality, explicitness, visible control flow, consistent conventions, and practical correctness over cleverness, minimal keystrokes, or fashion.
principles.source: `Primary Directive` (20-31), `Foundational Construction Rules` (35-42), `Forbidden Patterns / Cleverness over Clarity` (273-278), `Final Instruction` (345-354).

principles.directive: For complex routines, sketch precise pseudocode or intent comments, then keep only comments that still explain intent, constraints, contracts, or rationale.
principles.source: `Pseudocode Programming Process` (55-61), `Comment Rules` (207-213).

principles.directive: Keep routines cohesive, precisely named, small at the interface, and hard to misuse; separate setup, validation, computation, and side effects.
principles.source: `Routine Design Rules` (64-79), `Forbidden Patterns / Routine Bloat` (279-282), `Review Rules` (257-270).

principles.directive: Make variable and data meaning explicit through names, small scope, deliberate initialization, constants, stronger types, and visible units or sentinel meanings.
principles.source: `Variable and Data Rules` (82-95), `Data Type Rules` (98-107), `Code Generation Rules` (298-315).

principles.directive: Choose data types that make invalid or ambiguous values harder to represent; use booleans, enumerations, records, maps, and tables only where they clarify meaning.
principles.source: `Data Type Rules` (98-107), `Construction with Preconditions and Postconditions` (198-204).

principles.directive: Keep control flow simple enough to verify: shallow nesting, named predicates, clear normal path, clear loops, and no side-effect-dependent expressions or clever one-liners.
principles.source: `Control Flow Rules` (110-124), `Statement, Conditional, and Loop Rules` (127-136), `Forbidden Patterns / Cleverness over Clarity` (273-278), `Code Generation Rules` (298-315).

principles.directive: Use table-driven or data-driven logic for stable explicit mappings only when the table is clearer, obvious, synchronized with the rules, and validated.
principles.source: `Statement, Conditional, and Loop Rules` (127-136), `Table-Driven and Data-Driven Rules` (164-170), `Testing Rules` (318-324).

principles.directive: Validate input at trust boundaries; use assertions, invariant checks, and simple contracts for programmer assumptions, and validation or domain errors for expected external or business failures.
principles.source: `Defensive Programming Rules` (139-151), `Construction with Preconditions and Postconditions` (198-204), `Testing Rules` (318-324).

principles.directive: Handle errors at the right abstraction, preserve diagnostic context, standardize similar failures, keep the normal path readable, and never silently continue from corrupted or impossible state.
principles.source: `Defensive Programming Rules` (139-151), `Error Handling Rules` (154-161), `Final Instruction` (345-354).

principles.directive: Keep classes and modules focused, cohesive, and bounded by clear contracts; hide representation and internal bookkeeping, and avoid mixed concerns.
principles.source: `Class and Module Design Rules` (173-185), `Review Rules` (257-270), `Code Generation Rules` (298-315).

principles.directive: Treat rising complexity as defect risk: split tangled routines or modules, remove duplication that multiplies maintenance effort, and reduce maintainer working-memory load.
principles.source: `Complexity Management Rules` (188-195), `Review Checklist` (327-341).

principles.directive: Build in small, verifiable increments; integrate often enough to expose conflicts, keep partial work from rotting, and review and improve code during construction.
principles.source: `Incremental Construction Rules` (225-231), `Performance, Integration, Tools, and Craftsmanship` (245-254).

principles.directive: Match reviews, inspections, pair work, tests, static checks, and regression tests to defect risk; debug by reproducing, isolating, explaining, fixing, and verifying root causes.
principles.source: `Quality, Collaboration, Debugging, and Refactoring` (234-242), `Review Rules` (257-270), `Testing Rules` (318-324).

principles.directive: Refactor when structure hides intent, duplicates knowledge, or raises defect probability, and separate refactoring from behavior change when that improves reviewability.
principles.source: `Quality, Collaboration, Debugging, and Refactoring` (234-242).

principles.directive: Tune performance only when requirements and evidence justify it; measure before and after, and keep clarity unless an explicit measured tradeoff warrants the cost.
principles.source: `Performance, Integration, Tools, and Craftsmanship` (245-254).

principles.directive: Use tools, scripts, debuggers, profilers, editors, and build automation to reduce error-prone manual work, not to replace understanding.
principles.source: `Performance, Integration, Tools, and Craftsmanship` (245-254).

principles.directive: Use layout, comments, documentation, and coding standards to lower reader effort; prefer self-documenting structure first and keep comments accurate and purposeful.
principles.source: `Comment Rules` (207-213), `Coding Standards Rules` (216-222), `Performance, Integration, Tools, and Craftsmanship` (245-254), `Forbidden Patterns / Comment-as-Crutch` (288-290), `Forbidden Patterns / Consistency Neglect` (292-294).

principles.directive: When coding starts from a proposed solution, restate the requirement, architecture fit, risks, conventions, and success constraints.
principles.source: `Construction Prerequisites and Decisions` (45-52), `Review Checklist` (327-341).

principles.directive: When a routine is hard to name, mixes phases, has flag arguments, long parameters, or hidden side effects, redesign the interface or split the routine.
principles.source: `Routine Design Rules` (64-79), `Forbidden Patterns / Routine Bloat` (279-282), `Review Rules` (257-270).

principles.directive: When readers must decode units, ranges, precision, encoding, ownership, status, magic values, or primitive flags, move that meaning into names, constants, types, or structures.
principles.source: `Variable and Data Rules` (82-95), `Data Type Rules` (98-107).

principles.directive: When input crosses a trust boundary, decide what is validated, rejected, recovered from, asserted, and kept diagnosable.
principles.source: `Defensive Programming Rules` (139-151), `Error Handling Rules` (154-161), `Review Checklist` (327-341).

principles.directive: When branches, loops, recursion, exits, or exception paths become hard to verify, simplify before adding logic.
principles.source: `Control Flow Rules` (110-124), `Statement, Conditional, and Loop Rules` (127-136), `Code Generation Rules` (298-315).

principles.directive: When repeated branching maps stable categories, ranges, conversions, validation, dispatch, or configuration-like rules, consider a validated table.
principles.source: `Statement, Conditional, and Loop Rules` (127-136), `Table-Driven and Data-Driven Rules` (164-170).

principles.directive: When a class or module exposes representation, grows into a god object, or mixes unrelated responsibilities, restore the abstraction boundary.
principles.source: `Class and Module Design Rules` (173-185), `Review Rules` (257-270), `Forbidden Patterns` (273-295).

principles.directive: When tests cover only the happy path, add normal, boundary, invalid-input, defensive-check, routine-contract, and data-driven edge cases.
principles.source: `Testing Rules` (318-324), `Defensive Programming Rules` (139-151).

principles.directive: When debugging begins from a guess, first make the failure repeatable, collect evidence, isolate the path, and explain the cause.
principles.source: `Quality, Collaboration, Debugging, and Refactoring` (234-242).

principles.directive: When refactoring poorly understood or risky code, add tests or analysis first and keep behavior changes separate.
principles.source: `Quality, Collaboration, Debugging, and Refactoring` (234-242), `Testing Rules` (318-324).

principles.directive: When performance work begins, set a target, measure current behavior, change one thing, remeasure, and document any clarity tradeoff.
principles.source: `Performance, Integration, Tools, and Craftsmanship` (245-254).

principles.directive: When comments restate obvious mechanics or go stale, rewrite the code or delete the comment; when code cannot express intent, constraints, or usage, add a close accurate comment.
principles.source: `Comment Rules` (207-213), `Performance, Integration, Tools, and Craftsmanship` (245-254), `Forbidden Patterns / Comment-as-Crutch` (288-290).

principles.directive: When local style starts to diverge, follow shared formatting, naming, file-structure, and idiom conventions instead of creating a module-specific dialect.
principles.source: `Coding Standards Rules` (216-222), `Forbidden Patterns / Consistency Neglect` (292-294).

principles.directive: Make core data-system trade-offs explicit.
principles.source: `Purpose` (3-16), `Primary Directive` (20-32), `Final Instruction` (384-393).

principles.directive: Treat crashes, partial writes, duplicate work, timeouts, stale reads, and unknown downstream success as normal inputs; distinguish accepted, persisted, applied, and durable success.
principles.source: `Reliability Rules` (36-47), `Primary Directive` (25-32), `Final Instruction` (386-393).

principles.directive: Describe load and performance with concrete workload, bottleneck, contention, and tail-latency facts before changing architecture.
principles.source: `Scalability and Maintainability Rules` (50-58).

principles.directive: Choose data models, query models, and ownership boundaries from relationships, access patterns, consistency, update locality, evolution pressure, and primary-vs-derived data status.
principles.source: `Data Model and Storage Rules` (61-80), `Query Model and Data Shape Rules` (83-91), `API and Service Boundary Rules` (290-296).

principles.directive: Match storage engines, indexes, and analytical layouts to write/read/range/recovery needs, OLTP-vs-analytics fit, write amplification, and memory-vs-durability assumptions.
principles.source: `Storage Engine and Indexing Rules` (94-103).

principles.directive: Treat derived data as derived unless explicitly authoritative, with propagation, lag, observability, repair, and rebuild paths.
principles.source: `Data Model and Storage Rules` (65-80), `Derived Data Rules` (251-261), `Review Rules` (309-310), `Code Generation Rules` (342-345).

principles.directive: Define write semantics: durability, visibility, staleness, conflict detection, and conflict resolution.
principles.source: `Consistency Rules` (106-125), `Review Checklist` (369-375), `Final Instruction` (386-393).

principles.directive: Make commands, jobs, events, batch jobs, and stream processors safe under retry and replay; do not rely on unproved exactly-once delivery.
principles.source: `Reliability Rules` (38-47), `Idempotency and Replay Rules` (128-139), `Batch and Stream Processing Rules` (280-287), `Code Generation Rules` (338-352), `Testing Rules` (357-359).

principles.directive: Preserve only required ordering and scope it to the key, stream, partition, record, or entity history that defines correctness.
principles.source: `Primary Directive` (28, 32), `Ordering Rules` (142-157), `Review Rules` (301-307), `Review Checklist` (372).

principles.directive: Separate commands, events, durable logs, streams, and materialized views; design consumers and events for lag, duplicates, restart, replay, stable identifiers, metadata, and versioning.
principles.source: `Event, Log, and Stream Rules` (160-178), `Schema Evolution Rules` (181-193), `Review Rules` (302-310).

principles.directive: Design schemas, encodings, APIs, messages, events, and database changes as evolving contracts across mixed versions and migrations.
principles.source: `Schema Evolution Rules` (181-193), `Encoding and Data Flow Rules` (196-204), `API and Service Boundary Rules` (294-296), `Code Generation Rules` (344).

principles.directive: Choose replication topology from write topology, latency, failure tolerance, lag, failover, reconfiguration, conflict handling, read guarantees, quorum, and convergence needs.
principles.source: `Replication Rules` (221-229).

principles.directive: Partition by workload-relevant locality and consistency keys, with hot-key, skew, routing, secondary-index, rebalancing, and cross-partition costs explicit.
principles.source: `Partitioning and Locality Rules` (207-218), `Review Rules` (311).

principles.directive: Match transactions and isolation to invariants, atomicity scope, commit behavior, recovery, reconciliation, anomalies, and side-effect repair.
principles.source: `Transaction Rules` (232-248), `Testing Rules` (360), `Review Checklist` (375).

principles.directive: Treat distributed faults, clocks, timeouts, leases, locks, majorities, leadership, and coordination dependencies as assumptions needing a fault model.
principles.source: `Distributed Fault, Clock, and Consensus Rules` (265-275).

principles.directive: Use linearizability, total order broadcast, atomic commit, or consensus only where the coordination problem truly requires agreement.
principles.source: `Distributed Fault, Clock, and Consensus Rules` (272-274).

principles.directive: Make batch and stream processing recomputable and recoverable, with explicit time semantics, windows, late data, joins, checkpoints, external side effects, and source-to-sink guarantees.
principles.source: `Batch and Stream Processing Rules` (278-287), `Testing Rules` (362).

principles.directive: Align service boundaries with data ownership and update semantics; avoid casual splits of tightly consistent concepts and chatty cross-service joins.
principles.source: `API and Service Boundary Rules` (290-296), `Review Checklist` (377).

principles.directive: When changing a write path, state source of truth, consistency boundary, durability, visibility, downstream effects, rollback or repair, and unknown-success behavior.
principles.source: `Primary Directive` (25-30), `Reliability Rules` (38-47), `Consistency Rules` (114-125), `Transaction Rules` (232-248), `Review Rules` (309).

principles.directive: When adding or changing derived representations, define ownership, propagation, staleness, write cost, lag visibility, rebuild, and repair.
principles.source: `Data Model and Storage Rules` (65-80), `Storage Engine and Indexing Rules` (99-101), `Derived Data Rules` (251-261), `Review Checklist` (373).

principles.directive: When changing a schema, API, message, event, enum, status, or payload meaning, plan compatibility and migration across old readers, old writers, old data, old messages, new writers, and rollout.
principles.source: `Schema Evolution Rules` (181-193), `Encoding and Data Flow Rules` (198-203), `Testing Rules` (361).

principles.directive: When adding retries, jobs, consumers, queues, CDC, event sourcing, stream processors, or replayable batch work, prove duplicate, replay, ordering, retention, side-effect, and recovery safety.
principles.source: `Idempotency and Replay Rules` (128-139), `Event, Log, and Stream Rules` (160-178), `Batch and Stream Processing Rules` (278-287), `Testing Rules` (357-359).

principles.directive: When routing reads to replicas or using asynchronous replication, identify read-your-writes, monotonic-read, consistent-prefix, staleness, catch-up, failover, and conflict expectations.
principles.source: `Replication Rules` (221-229), `Consistency Rules` (108-125).

principles.directive: When partitioning data or work, test the ordinary query path for locality, skew, hot keys, routing metadata, rebalancing, secondary indexes, and cross-partition coordination.
principles.source: `Partitioning and Locality Rules` (207-218), `Review Rules` (311).

principles.directive: When choosing transaction isolation or weakening consistency, map each anomaly to the invariant it can break and add a compensating design where needed.
principles.source: `Transaction Rules` (232-248), `Consistency Rules` (106-125), `Testing Rules` (360).

principles.directive: When using timestamps, leases, locks, leadership, majority decisions, coordination services, or consensus-like mechanisms, define the clock assumption, quorum/session semantics, stale-authority behavior, and fencing.
principles.source: `Distributed Fault, Clock, and Consensus Rules` (265-275).

principles.directive: When reviewing or testing data-intensive code, scan for hidden ownership, missing idempotency, exactly-once assumptions, unscoped ordering, schema drift, unrebuildable projections, unclear multi-writes, and unobservable lag or failure.
principles.source: `Review Rules` (299-312), `Forbidden Patterns` (315-332), `Testing Rules` (355-363), `Review Checklist` (366-380).

principles.directive: Use a model only when it organizes domain knowledge, clarifies communication, and can be expressed in implementation; iterate through code, expert conversation, scenarios, and refactoring toward deeper insight.
principles.source: `Purpose` (3-17), `Primary Directive` (19-34), `What DDD Means in This Repository` (36-56), `Knowledge Crunching and Deep Models` (58-79), `Model-Driven Design` (81-101), `Breakthrough and Deeper Insight` (103-121), `Scenario Walkthroughs` (187-204).

principles.directive: Maintain one Ubiquitous Language per Bounded Context across names, tests, documents, diagrams, planning, and feature discussion; keep explanatory models separate from the implementation model.
principles.source: `Purpose` (3-17), `Model-Driven Design` (81-101), `Ubiquitous Language` (144-164), `Communication Artifacts` (167-184), `Testing Rules` (806-833), `Review Checklist` (943-968).

principles.directive: Put business logic in the domain layer. Keep UI, application coordination, infrastructure, persistence, messaging, and framework constraints outside the model or behind adapters.
principles.source: `Primary Directive` (19-34), `What DDD Means in This Repository` (36-56), `Layered Architecture and Smart UI` (207-220), `Application Layer` (593-615), `Infrastructure` (617-630), `Forbidden Patterns` (836-885), `Final Instruction` (970-979).

principles.directive: Use tactical patterns for model meaning: Entities for stable identity, Value Objects for immutable descriptive value, Services for important operations with no natural object home, and Modules for conceptual cohesion.
principles.source: `Entities` (386-411), `Value Objects` (414-440), `Associations and Modules` (443-467), `Domain Services` (496-517), `Code Generation Rules` (708-757), `Review Rules` (759-804), `Review Checklist` (943-968).

principles.directive: Manage lifecycle through Aggregates, Factories, and Repositories: expose only Aggregate roots, enforce invariants inside the boundary, hide complex creation and persistence, and prevent partially formed objects from escaping.
principles.source: `Purpose` (3-17), `Aggregates` (470-493), `Repositories` (542-569), `Factories` (572-592), `Code Generation Rules` (708-757), `Forbidden Patterns` (836-885), `Review Checklist` (943-968).

principles.directive: Design domain objects for the model first and persistence second; preserve identity, Aggregate boundaries, Value Object semantics, and domain query criteria instead of exposing database structure.
principles.source: `Primary Directive` (19-34), `Entities` (386-411), `Value Objects` (414-440), `Aggregates` (470-493), `Repositories` (542-569), `Infrastructure` (617-630), `Code Generation Rules` (708-757), `Final Instruction` (970-979).

principles.directive: Refactor toward deeper domain insight, not only mechanical cleanliness. Make constraints, policies, processes, calculations, allocations, and generation rules explicit when they carry domain meaning.
principles.source: `Knowledge Crunching and Deep Models` (58-79), `Breakthrough and Deeper Insight` (103-121), `Making Implicit Concepts Explicit` (124-140), `Explicit Concepts and Specifications` (520-539), `Supple Design` (653-678), `Refactoring Rules` (887-905), `Output Expectations` (908-941).

principles.directive: Design for model users: name operations by domain purpose, separate side-effect-free functions from state-changing commands, make assertions explicit, and shape boundaries around conceptual contours.
principles.source: `Ubiquitous Language` (144-164), `Entities` (386-411), `Explicit Concepts and Specifications` (520-539), `Supple Design` (653-678), `Forbidden Patterns` (836-885), `Review Checklist` (943-968).

principles.directive: Define every Bounded Context explicitly. Do not assume a term has the same meaning elsewhere; use context maps, tests, and active communication to protect model integrity.
principles.source: `Purpose` (3-17), `Bounded Contexts` (223-242), `Strategic Design` (245-266), `Model Integrity Patterns` (269-302), `Translation at Boundaries` (633-650), `Testing Rules` (806-833), `Review Checklist` (943-968).

principles.directive: Choose context relationships deliberately: Shared Kernel, Customer/Supplier, Conformist, Anticorruption Layer, Separate Ways, Open Host Service, Published Language, or incremental legacy replacement.
principles.source: `Strategic Design` (245-266), `Model Integrity Patterns` (269-302), `Translation at Boundaries` (633-650), `Code Generation Rules` (708-757), `Forbidden Patterns` (836-885), `Output Expectations` (908-941).

principles.directive: Distill and protect the Core Domain by strategic value. Keep generic subdomains, infrastructure, reusable mechanisms, and supporting details from consuming core-domain attention.
principles.source: `What DDD Means in This Repository` (36-56), `Strategic Design` (245-266), `Distillation` (305-331), `Strategic Decision Making` (363-383), `Code Generation Rules` (708-757), `Review Rules` (759-804), `Output Expectations` (908-941).

principles.directive: Add large-scale structure only when individual objects no longer make a large model understandable; keep structures domain-specific, evolvable, and valid only inside compatible contexts.
principles.source: `Large-Scale Structure` (334-360), `Strategic Decision Making` (363-383), `Review Rules` (759-804), `Review Checklist` (943-968).

principles.directive: Use analysis patterns, design patterns, specifications, industry formalisms, and prior art only when they clarify the current model and preserve domain language.
principles.source: `Making Implicit Concepts Explicit` (124-140), `Explicit Concepts and Specifications` (520-539), `Supple Design` (653-678), `Analysis and Model Patterns` (681-705).

principles.directive: Test the model in the Ubiquitous Language: prioritize domain tests for invariants, allowed and forbidden transitions, valid construction, specifications, application orchestration, and boundary translation before generic infrastructure checks.
principles.source: `Communication Artifacts` (167-184), `Scenario Walkthroughs` (187-204), `Testing Rules` (806-833), `Review Checklist` (943-968).

principles.directive: Make major strategic moves with people who understand both the implementation and the domain; architecture and framework guidance must serve application teams and domain goals.
principles.source: `Model-Driven Design` (81-101), `Strategic Decision Making` (363-383), `Review Rules` (759-804), `Output Expectations` (908-941).

principles.directive: Awkward, ambiguous, inconsistent, or repeatedly translated terminology triggers Ubiquitous Language refinement and code renaming.
principles.source: `Knowledge Crunching and Deep Models` (58-79), `Making Implicit Concepts Explicit` (124-140), `Ubiquitous Language` (144-164), `Review Rules` (759-804), `Refactoring Rules` (887-905).

principles.directive: Controllers, services, scripts, SQL, jobs, or serializers carrying business decisions trigger moving rules into domain objects, domain services, specifications, or explicit domain concepts.
principles.source: `Model-Driven Design` (81-101), `Layered Architecture and Smart UI` (207-220), `Domain Services` (496-517), `Explicit Concepts and Specifications` (520-539), `Application Layer` (593-615), `Review Rules` (759-804), `Forbidden Patterns` (836-885), `Refactoring Rules` (887-905).

principles.directive: UI, persistence, messaging, APIs, or frameworks shaping domain concepts triggers layers, adapters, translation, or an Anticorruption Layer.
principles.source: `Primary Directive` (19-34), `Layered Architecture and Smart UI` (207-220), `Repositories` (542-569), `Infrastructure` (617-630), `Translation at Boundaries` (633-650), `Forbidden Patterns` (836-885), `Final Instruction` (970-979).

principles.directive: A change crossing unrelated modules, many objects, or multiple roots triggers Module cohesion, Aggregate ownership, consistency timing, and context-boundary review.
principles.source: `Associations and Modules` (443-467), `Aggregates` (470-493), `Application Layer` (593-615), `Review Rules` (759-804), `Refactoring Rules` (887-905).

principles.directive: Client knowledge of creation, lifecycle, persistence, identity generation, or internal mutation details triggers repairs to Factories, Repositories, roots, and encapsulation.
principles.source: `Entities` (386-411), `Aggregates` (470-493), `Repositories` (542-569), `Factories` (572-592), `Forbidden Patterns` (836-885).

principles.directive: Behavior that is hard to explain, test, or extend triggers search for a deeper model, missing implicit concept, or breakthrough refactoring instead of adding procedural branches.
principles.source: `Knowledge Crunching and Deep Models` (58-79), `Breakthrough and Deeper Insight` (103-121), `Making Implicit Concepts Explicit` (124-140), `Scenario Walkthroughs` (187-204), `Explicit Concepts and Specifications` (520-539), `Refactoring Rules` (887-905).

principles.directive: Cross-model integration triggers explicit relationship, translation strategy, published language or protocol, and boundary tests before boundary code.
principles.source: `Bounded Contexts` (223-242), `Strategic Design` (245-266), `Model Integrity Patterns` (269-302), `Translation at Boundaries` (633-650), `Testing Rules` (806-833), `Forbidden Patterns` (836-885).

principles.directive: When changing invariants, lifecycle transitions, specifications, orchestration, or context translation, add domain-language tests that prove valid behavior and block invalid states.
principles.source: `Scenario Walkthroughs` (187-204), `Aggregates` (470-493), `Explicit Concepts and Specifications` (520-539), `Application Layer` (593-615), `Testing Rules` (806-833).

principles.directive: Generic mechanisms, reusable frameworks, or supporting subdomains obscuring distinctive value trigger Core Domain distillation or mechanism separation.
principles.source: `Strategic Design` (245-266), `Distillation` (305-331), `Large-Scale Structure` (334-360), `Strategic Decision Making` (363-383), `Forbidden Patterns` (836-885).

principles.directive: Identify capability, subdomain class, Bounded Context, Ubiquitous Language, and earned tactical patterns before designing code.
principles.source: `Purpose` (3-16), `Primary Directive` (20-35), `Adoption Fit and Modeling Investment` (38-45), `Code Generation Rules` (245-261), `Final Instruction` (308-317).

principles.directive: Put the most modeling effort into the Core Domain and keep Supporting and Generic Subdomains simpler unless complexity proves otherwise.
principles.source: `Purpose` (8-14), `Adoption Fit and Modeling Investment` (38-45), `Start with Subdomains` (50-60), `Practicality Rules` (219-230).

principles.directive: Avoid full tactical DDD for simple CRUD, generic subsystems, or mainly technical problems; strengthen the model only when real complexity rises.
principles.source: `Primary Directive` (20-35), `Adoption Fit and Modeling Investment` (38-45), `Practicality Rules` (219-230), `Review Rules` (264-278).

principles.directive: Give every meaningful model one explicit Bounded Context that owns language, rules, semantics, code structure, tests, and integration contracts.
principles.source: `Purpose` (8-14), `Primary Directive` (25-30), `Define Bounded Contexts Early` (61-66), `Use Context Mapping` (67-77), `Review Checklist` (291-304).

principles.directive: Treat same words across contexts as different until translated; avoid shared domain classes and foreign language leakage.
principles.source: `Define Bounded Contexts Early` (61-66), `Use Context Mapping` (67-77), `Ubiquitous Language Rules` (112-124).

principles.directive: Choose context relationships deliberately and honor their ownership and translation implications.
principles.source: `Use Context Mapping` (67-77), `Context Relationship Rules` (80-99), `Code Generation Rules` (245-261), `Review Rules` (264-278).

principles.directive: Select integration style from business coupling, latency, failure semantics, resource shape, messaging lag, duplicate handling, and ordering limits.
principles.source: `Context Relationship Rules` (80-99), `Integration Style Rules` (101-109).

principles.directive: Keep integration contracts separate from internal models and test translations across boundaries.
principles.source: `Use Context Mapping` (67-77), `Integration Style Rules` (101-109), `Architecture and Infrastructure Rules` (191-202), `Testing Rules` (281-288).

principles.directive: Use local domain terms consistently in code, tests, Commands, Domain Events, APIs, and conversations, and rename code when understanding improves.
principles.source: `Purpose` (8-14), `Ubiquitous Language Rules` (112-124), `Collaboration Rules` (205-216), `Testing Rules` (281-288).

principles.directive: Use Entities for explicit identity and lifecycle, and protect meaningful state transitions instead of exposing unrestricted setters.
principles.source: `Entities` (129-136), `Review Rules` (264-278), `Testing Rules` (281-288).

principles.directive: Use immutable, self-validating Value Objects when primitives hide domain meaning.
principles.source: `Value Objects` (137-144), `Collaboration Rules` (205-216), `Review Rules` (264-278), `Testing Rules` (281-288).

principles.directive: Use Aggregates only as invariant and transactional consistency boundaries; keep them small, root-protected, identity-referenced, and usually one Aggregate per transaction.
principles.source: `Aggregates` (145-153), `Aggregate Minimalism Rules` (162-174), `Review Checklist` (291-304).

principles.directive: Use Domain Events for meaningful past-tense business facts that clarify collaboration or integration, not noisy field changes.
principles.source: `Domain Events` (154-160), `Integration Style Rules` (101-109), `Review Checklist` (291-304).

principles.directive: Keep Application Services in orchestration: load Aggregates, invoke domain behavior, save, and trigger integration work without owning business decisions.
principles.source: `Application Service Rules` (177-188), `Code Generation Rules` (245-261), `Review Rules` (264-278), `Review Checklist` (291-304).

principles.directive: Keep frameworks, persistence, REST representations, transport formats, and infrastructure types out of the domain model.
principles.source: `Architecture and Infrastructure Rules` (191-202), `Integration Style Rules` (101-109), `Code Generation Rules` (245-261).

principles.directive: Write code that teaches the model by making assumptions explicit and exposing richer concepts instead of flags, status codes, booleans, enums, helpers, or utilities carrying domain meaning.
principles.source: `Ubiquitous Language Rules` (112-124), `Collaboration Rules` (205-216), `Review Rules` (264-278).

principles.directive: Use Event Storming, scenarios, acceptance tests, modeling spikes, and domain-expert walkthroughs when workflow, terminology, policies, or criteria are unclear; timebox modeling and track modeling debt.
principles.source: `Adoption Fit and Modeling Investment` (38-45), `Accelerated Modeling and Project Rules` (233-242), `Testing Rules` (281-288).

principles.directive: Estimate and plan DDD work from modeling uncertainty, integration risk, implementation cost, team skill, and expert access.
principles.source: `Accelerated Modeling and Project Rules` (233-242).

principles.directive: Fuzzy, generic, overloaded, or imported language triggers Ubiquitous Language refinement before more code.
principles.source: `Primary Directive` (25-30), `Ubiquitous Language Rules` (112-124), `Collaboration Rules` (205-216).

principles.directive: Core drift, code-language mismatch, or supporting complexity hiding the core triggers subdomain and boundary review.
principles.source: `Adoption Fit and Modeling Investment` (38-45), `Start with Subdomains` (50-60), `Practicality Rules` (219-230).

principles.directive: One model spreading across separate business concerns triggers splitting or translation instead of shared domain classes.
principles.source: `Use Context Mapping` (67-77), `Context Relationship Rules` (80-99), `Review Rules` (264-278).

principles.directive: Upstream model, schema, UI, framework, API payload, transport object, or database pressure triggers boundary translation.
principles.source: `Use Context Mapping` (67-77), `Integration Style Rules` (101-109), `Architecture and Infrastructure Rules` (191-202).

principles.directive: Shared Kernel requires small stable overlap, joint ownership, and tests.
principles.source: `Context Relationship Rules` (80-99).

principles.directive: A claimed Anticorruption Layer requires real translation.
principles.source: `Context Relationship Rules` (80-99).

principles.directive: Multi-root transactions, large graph loading, or graph mutation triggers Aggregate boundary and consistency timing review.
principles.source: `Aggregates` (145-153), `Aggregate Minimalism Rules` (162-174).

principles.directive: Controllers, helpers, services, or transport-shaped application services containing business decisions trigger moving behavior into the model or naming the missing concept.
principles.source: `Application Service Rules` (177-188), `Collaboration Rules` (205-216), `Review Rules` (264-278).

principles.directive: Vague, command-like, trivial, or field-change Domain Events trigger redesign as specific business facts or removal.
principles.source: `Domain Events` (154-160), `Review Rules` (264-278).

principles.directive: Primitives, flags, status codes, enums, or booleans carrying domain rules trigger richer concepts or Value Objects.
principles.source: `Value Objects` (137-144), `Collaboration Rules` (205-216), `Review Rules` (264-278).

principles.directive: Delivery pressure that would skip design triggers a short modeling spike, scenario, acceptance test, or explicit modeling debt.
principles.source: `Adoption Fit and Modeling Investment` (38-45), `Accelerated Modeling and Project Rules` (233-242), `Practicality Rules` (219-230).

principles.directive: Name the Bounded Context before interpreting model artifacts and reject a global company model.
principles.source: `Purpose` (3-17), `Primary Directive` (21-33), `Bounded Context Is Mandatory` (39-44), `Code Generation Rules` (261-270), `Final Instruction` (328-337).

principles.directive: Use one local Ubiquitous Language consistently across implementation artifacts and rename when understanding improves.
principles.source: `Purpose` (8-15), `Primary Directive` (25-33), `Ubiquitous Language Rules` (64-75), `Code Generation Rules` (261-264), `Review Checklist` (312-324), `Final Instruction` (330-337).

principles.directive: Protect the Core Domain, spend rich modeling only where complexity warrants it, keep supporting or CRUD areas simpler, and avoid DDD ceremony.
principles.source: `Core Domain Protection` (51-55), `Practical Simplicity Rule` (245-255).

principles.directive: Make context interactions explicit through visible relationships, translation responsibility, and upstream/downstream influence.
principles.source: `Context Mapping Is a Design Artifact` (45-50), `Context Integration Rules` (212-230).

principles.directive: Translate foreign, legacy, partner, external, and infrastructure models into local language and keep them out of local domain objects.
principles.source: `Primary Directive` (25-33), `Context Mapping Is a Design Artifact` (45-50), `Context Integration Rules` (214-230), `Client Representation and Scope Discipline` (236-241).

principles.directive: Treat Aggregates as small immediate consistency boundaries with one root, root-mediated invariant changes, hidden mutable internals, and intention-revealing behavior.
principles.source: `Aggregate Rules of Thumb` (78-90), anti-patterns (102-106), `Code Generation Rules` (264-267), `Review Rules` (284-294).

principles.directive: Reference other Aggregates by identity, avoid graph loading, and default to one Aggregate per transaction with eventual coordination outside the boundary.
principles.source: `Primary Directive` (25-33), `Reference Other Aggregates by Identity` (92-96), `One Aggregate per Transaction by Default` (97-100), aggregate anti-patterns (102-106), `Domain Event Rules` (161-165), `Final Instruction` (330-337).

principles.directive: Use Entities for identity, lifecycle, meaningful state transitions, and behavior rather than passive ORM containers.
principles.source: `Entities` (112-117).

principles.directive: Use immutable Value Objects for meaningful descriptive concepts, validate on construction, compare by value, and avoid primitive obsession for domain values.
principles.source: `Value Objects` (118-123), required behavior (124-126).

principles.directive: Use Domain or Transformation Services only for domain-significant operations or transformations that fit no model object, and keep technical mapping outside the domain.
principles.source: `Domain and Transformation Service Rules` (130-139).

principles.directive: Provide Repositories for Aggregate Roots with domain-oriented access contracts, not table CRUD, ORM rows, or business-rule implementations.
principles.source: `Repository Rules` (143-155).

principles.directive: Publish Domain Events only for meaningful completed business facts, use past-tense names, keep payloads model-local, and avoid property-change or command-like events.
principles.source: `Domain Event Rules` (159-166), event anti-patterns (174-178), `Review Rules` (284-294), `Review Checklist` (312-324).

principles.directive: Use Event Sourcing only when event streams are the right persistence model, with Aggregate identity, versioning, deterministic replay, and evolution support.
principles.source: `Event Sourcing` (167-172).

principles.directive: Keep Application Services as use-case coordinators that load Aggregates, invoke domain behavior, persist, publish events, and own transaction or integration coordination without holding core decision logic.
principles.source: `Application Service Rules` (182-193).

principles.directive: Organize modules by Bounded Context first and by domain or use-case ownership inside the context; avoid giant shared or common domain packages.
principles.source: `Module and Package Rules` (197-208), strategic anti-patterns (56-60).

principles.directive: Use DTOs, projections, use-case queries, rendition adapters, mediators, command/query separation, and explicit scope identifiers when client needs differ from Aggregate shape.
principles.source: `Client Representation and Scope Discipline` (234-241).

principles.directive: Follow the source code-generation order from context to language, tactical type, Aggregate boundary, identity references, invariants, repositories, Application Services, and translation layers.
principles.source: `Code Generation Rules` (259-278).

principles.directive: Test domain behavior and boundaries directly, including Aggregate invariants, transitions, Value Objects, events, repositories, translation layers, and Application Service orchestration.
principles.source: `Testing Rules` (298-306).

principles.directive: Ambiguous, reused, or technical-placeholder terms trigger qualification, splitting, or renaming before more code is written.
principles.source: `Ubiquitous Language Rules` (66-75), `Review Rules` (284-294).

principles.directive: Direct imports of another context's domain package, shared cross-context enums, or database coupling trigger explicit translation instead.
principles.source: strategic anti-patterns (56-60), `Context Integration Rules` (222-230), `Review Checklist` (312-324).

principles.directive: Legacy, vendor, partner, API, transport, persistence, UI, or foreign-context shapes in local code trigger an Anticorruption Layer or mapping boundary.
principles.source: `Context Mapping Is a Design Artifact` (45-50), `Domain and Transformation Service Rules` (134-139), `Context Integration Rules` (214-230), `Client Representation and Scope Discipline` (234-241).

principles.directive: Aggregate boundary changes or multi-Aggregate transactions trigger immediate-invariant proof; otherwise coordinate by identity, Domain Events, policies, processes, or Application Services.
principles.source: `Aggregate Rules of Thumb` (80-100), `Domain Event Rules` (161-165), `Application Service Rules` (184-188).

principles.directive: External mutation of Aggregate internals or read-decide-write behavior outside the root triggers movement behind intention-revealing root behavior.
principles.source: `Aggregate Root Discipline` (86-90), aggregate anti-patterns (102-106), `Review Rules` (284-294).

principles.directive: Generic CRUD, table-shaped, ORM-row-returning, or rule-owning Repository APIs trigger reshaping around Aggregate access.
principles.source: `Repository Rules` (143-155), `Code Generation Rules` (272-278), `Review Rules` (284-294).

principles.directive: Command-like events, property-change events, or events carrying framework request objects or persistence artifacts trigger renaming, narrowing, or removal.
principles.source: `Domain Event Rules` (159-166), event anti-patterns (174-178), `Review Rules` (284-294), `Review Checklist` (312-324).

principles.directive: Application Services or controllers accumulating branching business rules trigger moving decisions back into the domain model.
principles.source: `Application Service Rules` (182-193).

principles.directive: Client rendering, query speed, or representation pressure triggers DTOs, projections, use-case queries, or adapters instead of exposing or reshaping Aggregates.
principles.source: `Client Representation and Scope Discipline` (234-241), `Practical Simplicity Rule` (245-255).

principles.directive: Simple CRUD subdomains trigger simpler patterns, while real invariants and lifecycle complexity trigger honest modeling.
principles.source: `Practical Simplicity Rule` (245-255).

principles.directive: Make responsibility ownership explicit before naming patterns; one class or layer must not own presentation, workflow, domain, data source, transactions, concurrency, and integration.
principles.source: `Purpose` (3-16), `Primary Directive` (20-34).

principles.directive: Use layering by responsibility, with lower layers independent of presentation and no fake pass-through layers.
principles.source: `Architectural Baseline` (38-57).

principles.directive: Choose Transaction Script, Table Module, or Domain Model by business complexity, tabular shape, identity, lifecycle, and behavior needs.
principles.source: `Choosing the Business Logic Pattern` (60-94).

principles.directive: Keep each business logic pattern within its force: short scripts, honest table modules, and rich behavior in domain models; escalate when duplication, lifecycle, or invariant complexity grows.
principles.source: `Choosing the Business Logic Pattern` (62-94), `Code Generation Rules` (310-320).

principles.directive: Use Service Layer for application operations, orchestration, and transaction boundaries without absorbing all domain logic or exposing UI mechanics.
principles.source: `Application Workflow Rules` (97-107), `Code Generation Rules` (301-308).

principles.directive: Use Remote Facade and DTOs only at remote or layer/process boundaries, with coarse operations, batching, translation, and no domain behavior in DTOs.
principles.source: `Application Workflow Rules` (108-129), `Distribution Rules` (285-295), `Code Generation Rules` (306-308).

principles.directive: Choose Repository, Data Mapper, Row Data Gateway, Table Data Gateway, or Active Record by coupling and domain-separation pressure; repositories must speak domain terms and not become generic gateways.
principles.source: `Persistence Pattern Rules` (132-162), `Code Generation Rules` (305, 310-320).

principles.directive: Keep Identity Map, Unit of Work, and Lazy Load explicit so identity scope, logical commits, loading behavior, N+1 risk, hidden persistence, and ad hoc saves do not surprise callers.
principles.source: `Identity, Caching, and Unit-of-Work Rules` (166-185).

principles.directive: Choose object-relational mappings by identity, lifecycle, query need, schema shape, and evolution cost.
principles.source: `Object-Relational Mapping Pattern Index` (189-202).

principles.directive: Make concurrency and transaction policy explicit in the workflow: conflict detection, merge semantics, justified pessimism, short transactions, remote-call avoidance, and visible transaction ownership.
principles.source: `Concurrency and Transaction Rules` (206-228), `Review Checklist` (382, 389).

principles.directive: Use offline locks only when they preserve user-level consistency without hiding ownership, contention, or stale-lock diagnosis.
principles.source: `Concurrency and Transaction Rules` (225-228).

principles.directive: Keep presentation responsible for input, rendering, routing, formatting, pagination, UI state, and transport, not business rules.
principles.source: `Presentation Layer Rules` (232-248), `Review Rules` (326-329).

principles.directive: Keep external-system access behind boundaries, translate partner formats, and prevent integration messages, vendor DTOs, and serialization code from becoming domain design.
principles.source: `Offline and Integration Rules` (252-262).

principles.directive: Choose client, server, or database session state deliberately with security, scaling, cleanup, durability, sharing, and database-load costs explicit.
principles.source: `Session State and Base Pattern Index` (266-270).

principles.directive: Use base patterns only for concrete pressure: Gateway, Mapper, Layer Supertype, Separated Interface, Registry, Value Object, Money, Special Case, Plugin, Service Stub, and Record Set.
principles.source: `Session State and Base Pattern Index` (271-281).

principles.directive: Avoid distribution by default; when remote boundaries exist, make contracts coarse, separate them from local object design, and budget latency, serialization, versioning, and partial failure.
principles.source: `Distribution Rules` (285-295).

principles.directive: Generate code by first choosing the business logic pattern, then placing workflow, domain decisions, persistence, transactions, DTO/facade boundaries, and presentation concerns in their owning layers.
principles.source: `Code Generation Rules` (299-320).

principles.directive: Test behavior at the responsibility that owns it: domain, data-access infrastructure, service workflow, concurrency, and DTO/remote boundary mapping.
principles.source: `Testing Rules` (367-373).

principles.directive: Domain behavior in controllers, views, handlers, SQL scripts, triggers, DTOs, framework glue, serialization code, or vendor adapters triggers a responsibility move or explicit exception.
principles.source: `Architectural Baseline` (52-56), `Presentation Layer Rules` (232-248), `Offline and Integration Rules` (252-262), `Review Rules` (326-337).

principles.directive: One class or layer owning rendering, validation, SQL, transactions, domain rules, and external calls triggers a responsibility split.
principles.source: `Primary Directive` (25-34), `Forbidden Patterns` (354-363).

principles.directive: Transaction Script duplication, invariants, or lifecycle complexity triggers a Domain Model and persistence-pattern review.
principles.source: `Choosing the Business Logic Pattern` (62-94), `Review Rules` (330-331).

principles.directive: Table-shaped models in behavior-rich domains, generic CRUD repositories, or pass-through services trigger an ORM/database-ownership review.
principles.source: `Persistence Pattern Rules` (134-162), `Forbidden Patterns` (346-352), `Review Checklist` (383, 386).

principles.directive: Scattered SQL, mapping, transactions, locks, saves, or external resource access triggers the smallest centralizing repository, mapper, gateway, Unit of Work, service boundary, or policy.
principles.source: `Persistence Pattern Rules` (132-162), `Identity, Caching, and Unit-of-Work Rules` (166-185), `Concurrency and Transaction Rules` (219-228), `Session State and Base Pattern Index` (271-272), `Review Rules` (332-334).

principles.directive: Lazy-load surprises, duplicate identities, hidden auto-persistence, N+1 behavior, or ad hoc saves trigger explicit identity scope, Unit of Work, and loading behavior.
principles.source: `Identity, Caching, and Unit-of-Work Rules` (168-185), `Review Rules` (334-335), `Review Checklist` (389).

principles.directive: Concurrency conflicts, stale locks, user-level edits, or long-running workflows trigger explicit optimistic, pessimistic, coarse-grained, or implicit locking semantics.
principles.source: `Concurrency and Transaction Rules` (206-228), `Forbidden Patterns` (360-363), `Testing Rules` (372).

principles.directive: Chatty, object-shaped, or internals-leaking remote APIs trigger a coarse use-case contract with DTO translation.
principles.source: `Application Workflow Rules` (108-129), `Distribution Rules` (285-295), `Review Rules` (336-337).

principles.directive: Unclear session owner, lifetime, storage, security, scaling, failover, durability, or cleanup triggers an explicit session-state pattern choice.
principles.source: `Session State and Base Pattern Index` (266-270).

principles.directive: Layering theater, generic repository everywhere, ORM-driven everything, controller-centric enterprise workflow, distributed-object fantasy, or unclear transaction ownership blocks review until pattern ownership is corrected.
principles.source: `Forbidden Patterns` (341-363).

principles.directive: Preserve observable behavior and isolate behavior changes from structural changes.
principles.source: `Purpose` (3-16), `Primary Directive` (20-31), `What Counts as Refactoring` (35-49), `Non-Negotiable Rules` (53-58), `Forbidden Patterns` (329-342), `Review Checklist` (410-414).

principles.directive: Work in small reversible, buildable, testable, reviewable steps.
principles.source: `Primary Directive` (25-31), `Non-Negotiable Rules` (60-67), `Commit and Patch Discipline` (88-92), `Code Generation Rules` (360-369), `Final Instruction` (429-433).

principles.directive: Establish a safety net or characterization before risky refactoring and preserve failing tests.
principles.source: `Tests and Verification` (79-87), `Testing Rules` (388-395), `Forbidden Patterns` (353-357), `Review Checklist` (417).

principles.directive: Use preparatory and follow-up refactoring around feature work.
principles.source: `Primary Directive` (25-29), `Non-Negotiable Rules` (69-75), `Preparatory Refactoring` (94-100), `Code Generation Rules` (360-369).

principles.directive: Refactor the current blocking smell instead of detached polish.
principles.source: `Code Smell Policy` (104-155), `Review Rules` (310-325), `Review Checklist` (415-418).

principles.directive: Prefer the simplest named refactoring move that helps.
principles.source: `Preferred Refactoring Moves` (158-187), `Refactoring Catalog Index` (191-255), `Code Generation Rules` (371-379).

principles.directive: Make names and functions reveal intent with coherent tasks, consistent abstraction levels, tight scope, and separated phases.
principles.source: `Naming Refactorings` (160-165), `Extraction Refactorings` (166-170), `Long Functions` (113-117), `Function-Level Rules` (258-267), `Review Checklist` (421-422).

principles.directive: Put behavior and state with the owning concept and split modules by reasons to change.
principles.source: `Divergent Change` (128-130), `Shotgun Surgery` (132-134), `Feature Envy` (136-138), `Movement Refactorings` (172-175), `Moving Features` (204-212), `Generalization and Big Refactorings` (243-255), `Class and Module Rules` (270-278), `Review Checklist` (423).

principles.directive: Keep data, mutation, and call contracts explicit.
principles.source: `Long Parameter Lists` (118-122), `Data Clumps and Primitive Obsession` (140-143), `Data Refactorings` (183-187), `Composing Methods` (193-202), `Organizing Data` (213-225), `Simplifying Calls and Conditionals` (234-241), `Data and Mutation Rules` (291-298).

principles.directive: Simplify conditionals without introducing needless indirection.
principles.source: `Switch Statements and Conditionals` (144-147), `Simplification Refactorings` (177-181), `Simplifying Calls and Conditionals` (227-233), `Rules for Working with Conditionals` (281-288), `Review Checklist` (422).

principles.directive: Use abstraction, inheritance, delegation, and generalization only when current evidence justifies them.
principles.source: `Use the Simplest Helpful Refactoring` (73-75), `Middle Man and Speculative Generality` (152-155), `Generalization and Big Refactorings` (243-255), `Class and Module Rules` (274-277), `Forbidden Patterns` (343-351).

principles.directive: Preserve error semantics and refactor error handling to reveal the main path.
principles.source: `Simplifying Calls and Conditionals` (241), `Error Handling Rules` (301-307).

principles.directive: Keep patch intent reviewable and avoid giant mixed changes.
principles.source: `Commit and Patch Discipline` (88-92), `Forbidden Patterns` (338-342), `Code Generation Rules` (380-385), `Review Checklist` (420).

principles.directive: Stop when the requested change is easy, the blocking smell is removed, and further cleanup would be speculative.
principles.source: `Use the Simplest Helpful Refactoring` (73-75), `Stopping Rules` (399-406), `Review Checklist` (425), `Final Instruction` (429-433).

principles.directive: When adding behavior, decide whether a small preparatory refactor should come first.
principles.source: `Primary Directive` (25-29), `Preparatory Refactoring` (94-100), `Code Generation Rules` (360-369).

principles.directive: When fixing a bug in unclear code, characterize the failure and refactor only enough to make the fix visible before changing behavior.
principles.source: `Tests and Verification` (81-87), `Testing Rules` (388-395), `Code Generation Rules` (360-369).

principles.directive: When tests are absent or weak, make the smallest structural move and improve testability before broader cleanup.
principles.source: `Tests and Verification` (81-87), `Testing Rules` (388-395), `Untested Structural Surgery` (353-357).

principles.directive: When the same edit appears for a third time, remove duplication through clearer ownership.
principles.source: `Duplicated Code` (108-111), `Shotgun Surgery` (132-134), `Code Generation Rules` (371-379).

principles.directive: When a function mixes responsibilities, levels, phases, or hidden side effects, rename, extract, split phases, or isolate side effects.
principles.source: `Long Functions` (113-117), `Extraction Refactorings` (166-170), `Function-Level Rules` (258-267).

principles.directive: When one change spreads across many files, centralize knowledge or introduce a clearer boundary.
principles.source: `Shotgun Surgery` (132-134), `Divergent Change` (128-130), `Class and Module Rules` (270-278), `Review Rules` (319-325).

principles.directive: When repeated conditionals or type codes grow, decompose intent before introducing heavier variation mechanisms.
principles.source: `Switch Statements and Conditionals` (144-147), `Simplifying Calls and Conditionals` (227-233), `Rules for Working with Conditionals` (281-288).
