principles.directive: When UI and domain behavior mix, move rules toward domain objects and verify any required presentation synchronization.
principles.source: `Divergent Change` (128-130), `Data Refactorings` (183-187), `Generalization and Big Refactorings` (252-254), `Class and Module Rules` (272-273).

principles.directive: When a patch mixes intents or code motion makes review hard, split the change unless context makes that impractical.
principles.source: `Commit and Patch Discipline` (88-92), `Mixed-Intent Patches` (338-342), `Code Generation Rules` (380-385).

principles.directive: When tempted to rewrite, choose the next small behavior-preserving transformation.
principles.source: `Primary Directive` (20-31), `Big-Bang Rewrite` (333-337), `Final Instruction` (429-433).

principles.directive: Separate refactoring from feature work and bug fixes.
principles.source: `Primary Directive` (46-57), `Keep Refactoring Separate` (61-70), `Refactoring Workflow for Agents` (723-750).

principles.directive: Diagnose smell, cost, scope, expected end state, verification, and stop condition before choosing a technique.
principles.source: `Primary Directive` (46-57), `Smell Detection Process` (141-158), `Diagnose, Treat, Verify, Stop` (160-184), `Refactoring Workflow for Agents` (723-750).

principles.directive: Prefer the smallest direct treatment and escalate only when blocked.
principles.source: `Primary Directive` (46-57), `Diagnose, Treat, Verify, Stop` (160-184), `Smell-to-Treatment Priority Map` (497-523), `Safety and Tradeoff Rules` (708-719).

principles.directive: Work in small named transformations and avoid broad redesign.
principles.source: `Work in Small Steps` (72-79), `Technique Selection Rules` (411-493), `Technique Execution Safety` (631-704).

principles.directive: Verify continuously after risky structural changes.
principles.source: `Verify Continuously` (81-88), `Technique Playbook` (527-607), `Technique Execution Safety` (631-704), `Refactoring Workflow for Agents` (723-750).

principles.directive: Stop when the diagnosed smell is materially reduced; do not chase unrelated smells.
principles.source: `Diagnose, Treat, Verify, Stop` (160-184), `Decision Anti-Patterns` (611-627), `Refactoring Workflow for Agents` (723-750).

principles.directive: Use the Rule of Three without abstracting coincidental similarity.
principles.source: `Rule of Three` (99-106), `Duplicate Code` (325-333), `Decision Anti-Patterns` (611-627).

principles.directive: Treat technical debt as compounding cost and pay down debt blocking current change, correctness, or understanding.
principles.source: `Technical Debt Rules` (130-137), `When to Refactor` (99-126).

principles.directive: Scan smells by category before selecting treatment.
principles.source: `Smell Detection Process` (141-158), smell sections `Bloaters` through `Couplers` (199-407).

principles.directive: For bloaters, prefer extraction, modeling, and responsibility splits before heavier object or hierarchy moves.
principles.source: `Bloaters` (199-247), `Smell-to-Treatment Priority Map` (497-523), `Technique Playbook` (527-607).

principles.directive: For switch/type-code smells, isolate decisions first and use polymorphism or state/strategy only for stable repeated variation.
principles.source: `Switch Statements` (253-262), `Organizing Data` technique selection (436-452), `Conditional and Method Call Playbook` (572-595), `Decision Anti-Patterns` (611-627).

principles.directive: For change preventers, move behavior and data toward the owner of the changing concept.
principles.source: `Change Preventers` (290-312), `Moving Features Between Objects` (425-434), `Smell-to-Treatment Priority Map` (497-523).

principles.directive: For dispensables, delete or inline unused structure only after compatibility and external reachability checks.
principles.source: `Dispensables` (316-366), `Smell-to-Treatment Priority Map` (497-523), `Decision Anti-Patterns` (611-627).

principles.directive: For couplers, reduce navigation and private knowledge while preserving useful boundary layers.
principles.source: `Couplers` (370-407), `Moving Features Playbook` (543-552), `Safety and Tradeoff Rules` (708-719).

principles.directive: Prefer names, extraction, variables, or assertions over comments that explain unclear code; keep rationale and constraints.
principles.source: `Comments` (318-323), `Smell Exception Rules` (186-195), `Technique Selection Rules` (411-493).

principles.directive: Keep behavior with the data it changes unless separation intentionally supports interchangeable behavior.
principles.source: `Feature Envy` (372-378), `Moving Safety` (651-657), `Safety and Tradeoff Rules` (708-719).

principles.directive: Treat encapsulation as behavior and invariant protection, not just getters and setters.
principles.source: `Data Class` (342-348), `Encapsulation Safety` (659-665), `Decision Anti-Patterns` (611-627).

principles.directive: Avoid speculative abstractions and mechanical wrappers, parameter objects, interfaces, superclasses, or variants.
principles.source: `Speculative Generality` (359-366), `Decision Anti-Patterns` (611-627), `Safety and Tradeoff Rules` (708-719).

principles.directive: Preserve public compatibility or transition paths for externally reachable structure.
principles.source: `Technique Execution Safety` (631-704), `Safety and Tradeoff Rules` (708-719), `Refactoring Workflow for Agents` (723-750).

principles.directive: Before extraction or movement, check inputs, outputs, mutation, callers, visibility, construction, and invariants.
principles.source: `Technique Execution Safety` (631-657), `Technique Playbook` (527-607).

principles.directive: Before condition consolidation or algorithm substitution, check side effects, ordering, truth tables, edge cases, and performance-sensitive behavior.
principles.source: `Composing Methods Playbook` (531-541), `Conditional and Method Call Playbook` (572-595), `Conditional Safety` (667-674).

principles.directive: Before data reorganization, decide identity, value/reference semantics, mutability, equality, lifecycle, association direction, and synchronization.
principles.source: `Organizing Data` (436-452), `Organizing Data Playbook` (554-570), `Data Reorganization Safety` (686-694).

principles.directive: Before generalization changes, prove shared behavior is real and preserve substitutability.
principles.source: `Dealing With Generalization` (482-493), `Generalization Playbook` (597-607), `Generalization Safety` (696-704).

principles.directive: Choose exceptions deliberately when mechanical smell treatment worsens clarity.
principles.source: `Smell Exception Rules` (186-195), `Decision Anti-Patterns` (611-627), relevant smell `When to ignore` logic compressed throughout smell sections (199-407).

principles.directive: Long method trigger: extract named methods and handle locals before using a method object.
principles.source: `Long Method` (201-209), `Composing Methods Playbook` (531-541), `Extraction Safety` (633-641).

principles.directive: Large class trigger: extract responsibility; subclass/interface only for stable variants or real client subsets.
principles.source: `Large Class` (211-219), `Moving Features Playbook` (543-552), `Generalization Playbook` (597-607).

principles.directive: Primitive/type-code trigger: model concepts only when names, validation, behavior, or safer variation handling survive.
principles.source: `Primitive Obsession` (221-229), `Organizing Data Playbook` (554-570).

principles.directive: Parameter list trigger: replace derived parameters, preserve whole objects, or introduce parameter objects only for real concepts.
principles.source: `Long Parameter List` (231-237), `Data Clumps` (239-247), `Conditional and Method Call Playbook` (572-595).

principles.directive: Shotgun/change-preventer trigger: centralize knowledge with movement or extraction.
principles.source: `Change Preventers` (290-312), `Smell-to-Treatment Priority Map` (497-523).

principles.directive: Message-chain/middle-man trigger: hide delegates, move behavior, or remove pass-through layers.
principles.source: `Message Chains` (387-392), `Middle Man` (394-399), `Moving Features Playbook` (543-552).

principles.directive: Query/modifier trigger: split mutation from returned information unless atomic read-modify is the contract.
principles.source: `Simplifying Method Calls` (465-480), `Conditional and Method Call Playbook` (572-595), `Method Call Safety` (676-684).

principles.directive: Conditional trigger: decompose or consolidate branches only after checking side effects and ordering.
principles.source: `Simplifying Conditional Expressions` (454-463), `Conditional and Method Call Playbook` (572-595), `Conditional Safety` (667-674).

principles.directive: Null/error/assertion/value/reference/association trigger: verify semantics before changing structure.
principles.source: `Organizing Data Playbook` (554-570), `Conditional and Method Call Playbook` (572-595), `Data Reorganization Safety` (686-694).

principles.directive: Inheritance trigger: push members down or replace inheritance with delegation when inheritance creates refused bequest or intimacy.
principles.source: `Refused Bequest` (272-277), `Inappropriate Intimacy` (380-385), `Generalization Safety` (696-704).

principles.directive: Dead/speculative/library trigger: check external reachability and use the narrowest library extension.
principles.source: `Dead Code` (350-357), `Speculative Generality` (359-366), `Incomplete Library Class` (401-407).

principles.directive: Cleanup-spread trigger: stop at the diagnosed smell and report the next smell separately.
principles.source: `Diagnose, Treat, Verify, Stop` (160-184), `Decision Anti-Patterns` (611-627).

principles.directive: Assume dependencies, queues, caches, timeouts, callers, and degraded states can fail slowly, partially, or for hours.
principles.source: `Purpose` (3-18), `Stability Mindset Rules` (36-45).

principles.directive: Prefer visible failure, blast-radius limits, load shedding, core-service preservation, and diagnosability over coupled happy-path design.
principles.source: `Primary Directive` (21-32), `Final Instruction` (373-382).

principles.directive: Treat deployment, operations, security, observability, rollback, state visibility, and configuration validation as part of production readiness.
principles.source: `Purpose` (8-17), `Production Readiness and Release Risk Rules` (49-55).

principles.directive: Put explicit, intentional time limits on outbound calls and waits; forbid infinite waits where finite response matters.
principles.source: `Dependency Protection Rules / Timeouts Are Mandatory` (59-66), `Code Generation Rules` (323-340), `Review Checklist` (355-360).

principles.directive: Retry only when safe, bounded, backed off or jittered, and not used for validation or permanent failures.
principles.source: `Dependency Protection Rules / Retries Must Be Disciplined` (67-72), `Forbidden Patterns / Retry Storms` (302-305), `Scheduled and Background Work Rules` (270-276), `Review Checklist` (355-360).

principles.directive: Isolate dependency and workload failures with breakers, fast failure, bulkheads, separate pools, and slow-work isolation.
principles.source: `Dependency Protection Rules / Circuit Breakers and Fast Failure` (73-77), `Dependency Protection Rules / Bulkheads and Isolation` (78-88), `Forbidden Patterns / Blast-Radius Amplification` (317-320), `Review Checklist` (355-367).

principles.directive: Design overload behavior with back pressure, finite queues, demand limits, critical capacity reservation, and load shedding.
principles.source: `Load and Capacity Rules` (91-126), `Forbidden Patterns / Collapse by Queue` (307-310), `Review Checklist` (355-367).

principles.directive: Choose stability patterns by failure mode: steady state, fail fast, let-it-crash only with supervision and isolation, handshaking, monitored decoupling middleware, and governors.
principles.source: `Load and Capacity Rules / Additional Stability Patterns` (114-120).

principles.directive: Make runtime state, external responses, automation progress, migrations, operational assumptions, and boundary data visible and validated, with rollback or roll-forward for partial changes.
principles.source: `Runtime State and Restart Safety Rules` (129-148), `Deployment and Startup Rules` (224-230).

principles.directive: Budget and release scarce resources, avoid holding locks or expensive connections across slow remote calls, and stream or paginate large payloads.
principles.source: `Resource Management Rules` (151-169).

principles.directive: Validate external input and responses before trusting them, and prevent malformed data from poisoning caches, queues, or downstream systems.
principles.source: `Runtime State and Restart Safety Rules` (131-135), `Data Boundary Rules` (172-179).

principles.directive: Build observability into boundaries and failure points with structured logs, correlation, metrics, health, dependency, version, configuration, runtime, saturation, queue, retry, and breaker signals.
principles.source: `Operational Visibility Rules` (182-211), `Review Rules` (280-293), `Review Checklist` (355-367).

principles.directive: Make startup, health checks, migrations, one-time jobs, administrative controls, process code, and delivery tooling safe, authorized, auditable, observable, stoppable, and recoverable.
principles.source: `Incidents, Capacity, and Runtime Control` (214-220), `Deployment and Startup Rules` (224-230), `Code Generation Rules` (323-340), `Testing Rules` (344-351).

principles.directive: Make interconnects, routing, API contracts, caches, scheduled work, and background work production-aware by avoiding concentrated demand, hidden SPOFs, uncontrolled fan-out, fragile chattiness, cache dogpiles, stale data surprises, and synchronized retries.
principles.source: `Interconnect, Routing, Security, and Chaos Rules` (234-242), `API and Contract Rules` (246-252), `Cache Rules` (256-266), `Scheduled and Background Work Rules` (270-276).

principles.directive: Include security and hostile traffic in production readiness, and run production tests, game days, chaos, or disaster simulations only with limited blast radius, observability, stop conditions, recovery, and design feedback.
principles.source: `Interconnect, Routing, Security, and Chaos Rules` (234-242), `Incidents, Capacity, and Runtime Control` (217-220), `Testing Rules` (344-351).

principles.directive: Outbound calls, dependency operations, resource checkouts, queue consumes, or thread waits trigger timeout, retry eligibility, retry bounds, fallback or degraded mode, validation, and caller-survival decisions.
principles.source: `Dependency Protection Rules` (59-88), `Runtime State and Restart Safety Rules` (131-135), `Code Generation Rules` (323-340).

principles.directive: Queues, buffers, pools, caches, log streams, background jobs, scheduled jobs, or collection APIs trigger capacity, full behavior, cleanup, miss/stampede/staleness behavior, pacing, pagination or streaming, and saturation monitoring.
principles.source: `Load and Capacity Rules` (91-126), `Resource Management Rules` (151-169), `Cache Rules` (256-266), `Scheduled and Background Work Rules` (270-276).

principles.directive: Deployment, configuration, startup, migrations, one-time jobs, scripts, or operational automation trigger idempotency or restartability, durable state, auditability, verification, and rollback or roll-forward.
principles.source: `Production Readiness and Release Risk Rules` (49-55), `Runtime State and Restart Safety Rules` (129-148), `Deployment and Startup Rules` (224-230).

principles.directive: Health checks, load balancing, service discovery, routing, or inter-service handshakes trigger readiness-aware traffic routing and health signals that reflect real ability to serve.
principles.source: `Load and Capacity Rules / Additional Stability Patterns` (114-120), `Deployment and Startup Rules` (224-230), `Interconnect, Routing, Security, and Chaos Rules` (234-242).

principles.directive: API and integration contracts trigger explicit failure modes, retryable/non-retryable outcomes, resilient interaction granularity, versioning, compatibility, and documented timeout or retry expectations.
principles.source: `API and Contract Rules` (246-252).

principles.directive: Incidents, performance failures, or capacity issues trigger failure-chain analysis, missing-defense review, detection-gap review, demand and saturation inspection, queue-age and dependency review, traffic-concentration review, and design changes.
principles.source: `Incidents, Capacity, and Runtime Control` (214-220).

principles.directive: Administrative controls, control planes, delivery tooling, hostile-traffic handling, or chaos/disaster work trigger authorization, auditability, safe defaults, stop mechanisms, bounded blast radius, and recovery paths.
principles.source: `Incidents, Capacity, and Runtime Control` (214-220), `Interconnect, Routing, Security, and Chaos Rules` (234-242).

principles.directive: Be pragmatic, not dogmatic; choose practices and stopping points by real outcomes.
principles.source: `Purpose` (3-17), `Primary Directive` (21-32), `Core Pragmatic Principles` (53-61), `Project and Team Rules` (248-255), `Forbidden Patterns` (283-287).

principles.directive: Own the result and surface tradeoffs, risks, uncertainty, and avoidable design costs.
principles.source: `Purpose` (3-17), `Core Pragmatic Principles` (38-42), `Project and Team Rules` (248-255), `Review Rules` (268-280).

principles.directive: Think beyond the local edit and leave touched areas better where the cost is low.
principles.source: `Core Pragmatic Principles` (43-47), `Broken Windows Rule` (259-264), `Review Checklist` (334-346), `Final Instruction` (350-359).

principles.directive: Keep one authoritative representation for each piece of system knowledge.
principles.source: `Purpose` (8-15), `Primary Directive` (25-32), `DRY Rules` (64-79), `Code Generation Rules` (306-322), `Review Checklist` (334-346), `Final Instruction` (350-359).

principles.directive: Preserve orthogonality with independent components, narrow interfaces, and separated concerns.
principles.source: `Purpose` (8-15), `Primary Directive` (25-32), `Orthogonality Rules` (82-93), `Resource and Coupling Rules` (235-244), `Review Rules` (268-280), `Final Instruction` (350-359).

principles.directive: Keep volatile decisions reversible until evidence justifies commitment.
principles.source: `Core Pragmatic Principles` (48-52), `Reversibility, Domain Languages, and Requirements` (110-120), `Text and Data Rules` (189-195), `Final Instruction` (350-359).

principles.directive: Use domain vocabulary and small domain languages only when they clarify rules for their readers.
principles.source: `Reversibility, Domain Languages, and Requirements` (110-120), `Naming and Communication Rules` (179-185).

principles.directive: Prefer thin end-to-end tracer bullets to validate architecture, integration, and assumptions early.
principles.source: `Purpose` (8-15), `Tracer Bullets and Iterative Delivery` (96-107), `Code Generation Rules` (306-322).

principles.directive: Use prototypes for learning while preventing experimental shortcuts from becoming production defaults.
principles.source: `Tracer Bullets and Iterative Delivery` (103-107), `Prototyping Rules` (123-129), `Forbidden Patterns` (301-303), `Review Checklist` (334-346).

principles.directive: Dig for real requirements and separate durable needs from implementation details, solution proposals, stale specs, and unresolved hesitation.
principles.source: `Reversibility, Domain Languages, and Requirements` (110-120), `Estimation and Increment Rules` (207-213).

principles.directive: Automate repetitive, error-prone, easy-to-forget, or ritualized work and keep shared checks reproducible.
principles.source: `Purpose` (8-15), `DRY Rules` (72-77), `Automation Rules` (132-143), `Tooling Rules` (216-231), `Forbidden Patterns` (285-299), `Code Generation Rules` (306-322), `Testing Rules` (325-331), `Review Checklist` (334-346), `Final Instruction` (350-359).

principles.directive: Shorten feedback loops with relevant tests, automated checks, visible failures, and cheap early signals.
principles.source: `Purpose` (8-15), `Primary Directive` (25-32), `Feedback Loop Rules` (146-153), `Code Generation Rules` (306-322), `Testing Rules` (325-331), `Review Checklist` (334-346), `Final Instruction` (350-359).

principles.directive: Make contracts, assumptions, invariants, responsibilities, and obligations explicit and close to the protected abstraction.
principles.source: `Design by Contract and Assertions` (156-167), `Code Generation Rules` (306-322), `Review Checklist` (334-346).

principles.directive: Separate failure types, preserve diagnostic context, and contain failure boundaries.
principles.source: `Design by Contract and Assertions` (156-167), `Error Handling and Recovery` (170-176), `Review Rules` (268-280).

principles.directive: Treat resource ownership and cleanup as explicit contracts on success and failure paths.
principles.source: `Resource and Coupling Rules` (235-244), `Review Checklist` (334-346).

principles.directive: Prefer inspectable text, open formats, scripts, explicit serialization, and version-aware configuration where longevity and automation matter.
principles.source: `Text and Data Rules` (189-195), `Tooling Rules` (223-231).

principles.directive: Treat shared mutable state, ambient context, globals, temporal coupling, and async complexity as visible costs.
principles.source: `Orthogonality Rules` (82-93), `State and Concurrency Rules` (198-204), `Resource and Coupling Rules` (235-244).

principles.directive: Use tools as leverage, but understand generated code, formal methods, specifications, and tool output before relying on them.
principles.source: `Tooling Rules` (216-231), `Forbidden Patterns` (285-303).

principles.directive: Debug from reproduced facts by observing, isolating, explaining, fixing, and verifying.
principles.source: `Tooling Rules` (230-231).

principles.directive: Break work into small deliverable increments with honest uncertainty, visible risk, and estimates corrected by feedback.
principles.source: `Tracer Bullets and Iterative Delivery` (96-107), `Estimation and Increment Rules` (207-213).

principles.directive: Communicate engineering intent through code, names, docs, comments, commit messages, scripts, tests, and artifacts.
principles.source: `Core Pragmatic Principles` (53-61), `Naming and Communication Rules` (179-185), `Project and Team Rules` (248-255), `Review Rules` (268-280).

principles.directive: Build teams and work around shared responsibility, explicit expectations, automation, fast feedback, visible quality, and defensible artifacts.
principles.source: `Project and Team Rules` (248-255).

principles.directive: Apply the broken windows rule by fixing or visibly containing quality decay.
principles.source: `Core Pragmatic Principles` (53-61), `Broken Windows Rule` (259-264), `Review Checklist` (334-346).

principles.directive: Copied facts trigger source-of-truth ownership and derivation, generation, validation, or traceability.
principles.source: `DRY Rules` (64-79), `Forbidden Patterns` (289-292), `Review Rules` (268-280), `Review Checklist` (334-346).

principles.directive: Unrelated places changing together trigger boundary repair and hidden-coupling removal.
principles.source: `Orthogonality Rules` (82-93), `Forbidden Patterns` (293-295), `Review Rules` (268-280).

principles.directive: Hard-coded volatile details trigger validated, controlled, versioned configuration, metadata, or an explicit abstraction.
principles.source: `Reversibility, Domain Languages, and Requirements` (110-120), `Text and Data Rules` (189-195).

principles.directive: High uncertainty or irreversible decisions trigger tracer feedback, prototypes, smaller reversible steps, or delayed commitment.
principles.source: `Core Pragmatic Principles` (48-52), `Tracer Bullets and Iterative Delivery` (96-107), `Reversibility, Domain Languages, and Requirements` (110-120), `Estimation and Increment Rules` (207-213).

principles.directive: Prototype code, generated scaffolds, diagrams, specs, formal models, or tool output becoming production truth triggers deliberate inspection, understanding, hardening, replacement, or rejection.
principles.source: `Tracer Bullets and Iterative Delivery` (103-107), `Prototyping Rules` (123-129), `Tooling Rules` (229-231), `Forbidden Patterns` (301-303).

principles.directive: Prose specifications growing without reduced uncertainty trigger a working slice, example, or prototype.
principles.source: `Reversibility, Domain Languages, and Requirements` (116-119), `Tracer Bullets and Iterative Delivery` (96-107).

principles.directive: Hidden assumptions in comments, caller folklore, or setup rituals trigger code, contracts, tests, scripts, or checked configuration.
principles.source: `Design by Contract and Assertions` (156-167), `Automation Rules` (132-143), `Review Rules` (268-280).

principles.directive: Errors or resources crossing boundaries trigger recovery, diagnostic-context, and cleanup ownership decisions.
principles.source: `Error Handling and Recovery` (170-176), `Resource and Coupling Rules` (235-244).

principles.directive: Shared state, async behavior, locks, ordering, or temporal coupling trigger explicit ownership, synchronization, cleanup, and ordering requirements.
principles.source: `State and Concurrency Rules` (198-204), `Resource and Coupling Rules` (235-244).

principles.directive: Repeated manual steps, human checks, environment rituals, or release procedures trigger automation and versioning.
principles.source: `DRY Rules` (72-77), `Automation Rules` (132-143), `Forbidden Patterns` (285-299).

principles.directive: Slow, flaky, environment-dependent, or setup-heavy tests trigger feedback-path improvement instead of skipped checks.
principles.source: `Feedback Loop Rules` (146-153), `Testing Rules` (325-331).

principles.directive: Human-found bugs trigger automatic regression tests around the protected contract.
principles.source: `Testing Rules` (325-331), `Feedback Loop Rules` (146-153).

principles.directive: Unexplained working behavior triggers proof with data before dependency.
principles.source: `Tooling Rules` (230-231), `Design by Contract and Assertions` (156-167).

principles.directive: Local decay in touched code triggers a cheap fix or explicit containment and cleanup path.
principles.source: `Core Pragmatic Principles` (43-47), `Broken Windows Rule` (259-264), `Review Checklist` (334-346).

principles.directive: Treat any area without trustworthy tests as legacy code; avoid rewrite-first and module-wide cleanup.
principles.source: `Purpose` (3-16), `Primary Directive` (20-35), `Non-Negotiable Rules` (39-58), `Default Workflow for Legacy Changes` (61-74), `Forbidden Patterns` (286-305), `Final Instruction` (362-371).

principles.directive: State the behavior delta and characterize uncertain, suspicious, or ugly current behavior before modification.
principles.source: `Primary Directive` (20-35), `Non-Negotiable Rules` (39-58), `Testing Strategy Rules` (78-99), `Forbidden Patterns` (286-305), `Review Checklist` (345-358).

principles.directive: Follow the legacy loop from change point through seam, dependency break, test, change, and local refactor.
principles.source: `Primary Directive` (20-35), `Default Workflow for Legacy Changes` (61-74), `Code Generation Rules` (308-332), `Final Instruction` (362-371).

principles.directive: Prefer fast focused tests; use broader interception or integration tests only as the safest first observation point.
principles.source: `Testing Strategy Rules` (78-99), `Test Selection and Understanding Rules` (164-174), `Testing Rules` (335-342).

principles.directive: Choose test points by tracing effects from the change point through values, calls, fields, outputs, collaborators, interception points, and pinch points.
principles.source: `Test Selection and Understanding Rules` (164-174).

principles.directive: Use the smallest seam for substitution, observation, or interception, with sensing vs separation explicit.
principles.source: `Seam Rules` (102-125), `Review Checklist` (345-358).

principles.directive: Break dependencies deliberately only where hidden inputs, hard outputs, hard construction, globals, statics, ambient context, or framework callbacks block testing or safe change.
principles.source: `Non-Negotiable Rules` (39-58), `Dependency Breaking Rules` (128-161), `Handling Risky Areas` (240-267), `Forbidden Patterns` (286-305).

principles.directive: Keep behavior changes, refactorings, and cleanup separate; verify small steps and discard unsupported exploratory restructuring.
principles.source: `Test Selection and Understanding Rules` (164-174), `Legacy Refactoring Heuristics` (228-237), `Review Rules` (271-283), `Review Checklist` (345-358).

principles.directive: Use sprout, wrap, and extract-and-override style moves for risky additions, then fold temporary structures into better design under tests.
principles.source: `Preferred Legacy Techniques` (178-205), `Code Generation Rules` (308-332).

principles.directive: For hard-to-test methods, split construction from use, extract side effects, carve pure computation first, and isolate policy from runtime, persistence, UI, or framework mechanisms.
principles.source: `Dependency Breaking Rules` (128-161), `Legacy Refactoring Heuristics` (228-237), `Handling Risky Areas` (240-267), `Code Generation Rules` (308-332).

principles.directive: Match dependency-breaking techniques to the actual barrier and use link/preprocessing seams only when ordinary object seams are impractical.
principles.source: `Seam Rules` (102-125), `Dependency-Breaking Technique Index` (208-224), `Code Generation Rules` (308-332).

principles.directive: In large code, sketch effects and group responsibilities before moving behavior; let setup pain, impossible observation, and repeated changes point to smaller responsibilities.
principles.source: `Test Selection and Understanding Rules` (164-174), `Legacy Refactoring Heuristics` (228-237), `Handling Risky Areas` (240-267).

principles.directive: Review legacy changes for missing tests, mixed edit intent, broad edits, hard-coded collaborators, global/static reach-through, constructor side effects, and framework-trapped business logic.
principles.source: `Review Rules` (271-283), `Review Checklist` (345-358).

principles.directive: Reject hidden dependency expansion, mocking around untestable structure without improving it, cosmetic cleanup, and large architecture before basic seams exist.
principles.source: `Forbidden Patterns` (286-305), `Code Generation Rules` (308-332).

principles.directive: Leave the touched area easier to understand, test, or change without mistaking temporary seams or build tricks for design improvement.
principles.source: `Primary Directive` (20-35), `Non-Negotiable Rules` (39-58), `Seam Rules` (102-125), `Review Checklist` (345-358), `Final Instruction` (362-371).

principles.directive: Uncertain behavior, consumer reliance on ugly behavior, or hard-to-prove paths trigger characterization or another explicit observation path.
principles.source: `Testing Strategy Rules` (78-99), `Forbidden Patterns` (286-305).

principles.directive: Excessive test setup or instantiation failure triggers breaking the first real barrier: constructor work, hidden allocation, factory call, global/static state, framework object, or hard parameter.
principles.source: `Dependency Breaking Rules` (128-161), `Dependency-Breaking Technique Index` (208-224), `Handling Risky Areas` (240-267).

principles.directive: Time, randomness, environment, thread-local state, user/request context, files, networks, process exits, database writes, messages, or control-flow logging that block repeatable tests trigger wrapping or injection.
principles.source: `Dependency Breaking Rules` (128-161), `Handling Risky Areas` (240-267).

principles.directive: Large methods or classes that defeat local reasoning trigger effect sketches, interception or pinch points, pure-computation extraction, and branch-count restraint.
principles.source: `Test Selection and Understanding Rules` (164-174), `Handling Risky Areas` (240-267).

principles.directive: Database-heavy, UI, framework, or API-boundary code triggers separating policy from boundary mechanisms while keeping real-boundary integration tests where they matter.
principles.source: `Handling Risky Areas` (240-267), `Testing Rules` (335-342).

principles.directive: Magical, temporary, public-for-test, subclass-only, link/preprocessor, or probe/sensing seams trigger cleanup obligations.
principles.source: `Testing Strategy Rules` (78-99), `Seam Rules` (102-125), `Preferred Legacy Techniques` (178-205), `Dependency-Breaking Technique Index` (208-224).

principles.directive: Repeated edits across places trigger incremental duplication removal under tests rather than broad redesign.
principles.source: `Legacy Refactoring Heuristics` (228-237), `Final Instruction` (362-371).

principles.directive: Rewrite or heroic-cleanup pressure triggers the smallest sprout, wrap, seam, characterization, or refactoring step that makes today's change safer.
principles.source: `Primary Directive` (20-35), `Default Workflow for Legacy Changes` (61-74), `Forbidden Patterns` (286-305), `Final Instruction` (362-371).
